title: How we became Top Rated on Upwork (and why the platform isn't dead)
subheading: Won a major project against a 50+ person agency through strategic positioning, founder-level thinking, and world-class delivery
excerpt: Learn how we achieved Top Rated status on Upwork with 100% success score by standing out with quality, strategic positioning, and world-class client experience.
seoDescription: Learn how we achieved Top Rated status on Upwork with 100% success score by standing out with quality, strategic positioning, and world-class client experience.
coverAlt: Top Rated Upwork badge for Eterna Creative showing 100% job success score and 5-star ratings for no-code Bubble.io

toc.text: The real problem isn't Upwork - it's your approach
1. Perfect your profile (it's your first pitch)
3. Pitch concrete value, not generic skills
4. Deliver world-class experience from start to finish
Why we beat the 50+ person agency
Upwork isn't dead - it's just selective

bodyHtml:
TL;DR

  • Achieved Top Rated status on Upwork with 100% Job Success Score and 5-star ratings
  • Won a major project against a 50+ person agency through better approach and founder-level guidance
  • Upwork isn't dead - you just need to stand out with quality delivery and strategic positioning
  • Free App Launch Blueprint upfront proves capability before clients spend a dollar

"Don't waste your time on Upwork"

That's what everyone told me. "The platform is dead. It's all a race to the bottom. You'll compete with $5/hour developers from countries with low costs. Clients just want cheap, not quality." I had the same feeling and for months, I avoided Upwork completely. Then my friend - who runs a 20+ person SEO agency and crushes it on Upwork - told me I was being stupid. "You're leaving money on the table because you're scared of competition. Just try it." So I did.

Two months later: Top Rated badge, 100% Job Success Score, 5-star rating. And we just won a major project (announcement coming soon) against a 50+ person agency. Here's my take on what actually works on Upwork in 2025.

The real problem isn't Upwork - it's your approach

Most developers treat Upwork like a job board. They spray generic proposals hoping something sticks. That's why they fail. Here's what we did differently:

1. Perfect your profile (it's your first pitch)

Your profile isn't a resume. It's a sales page. We positioned ourselves clearly:

  • Bubble.io developer | 20+ apps built | MVP | SaaS | Fundable products
  • Higher range rate (not competing on price)
  • Portfolio showing real projects with context
  • Clear specialization (no-code for startups)
The result: Clients who message us already understand what we do and why we cost more.

2. Choose projects, don't chase them

Better to skip a project than bang your head against a nightmare client.

Red flags we watch for:
  • Vague requirements with "just a simple app" language
  • Budget under $1K for complex scope
  • "Looking for the cheapest option" in description
  • Multiple revisions or scope changes in previous feedback
  • Demanding tone or unrealistic timeline expectations
Green flags we look for:
  • Clear project description with thought-through requirements
  • Realistic budget for scope described
  • Questions that show they understand development isn't magic
  • Previous jobs with good feedback given
  • Professional communication style

We turn down 70% of invitations. The 30% we take convert at high rates because they're already qualified.

3. Pitch concrete value, not generic skills

Every developer says "I'm experienced in Bubble" or "I can build your app." That's noise.

Our proposals:
  • Start with the client's specific pain point or challenge
  • Show we understand their business context, not just tech requirements
  • Reference similar projects we've built with outcomes
  • Position as strategic partner, not just hands

This demonstrates industry leadership, not just technical capability.

4. Deliver world-class experience from start to finish

Quality delivery is obvious. But client process matters just as much. Our process:

  • Start every project with free App Launch Blueprint (hero screen design, user flow map, technical roadmap, strategic analysis)
  • Weekly demo calls showing progress (no surprises at delivery)
  • Notion client portal for all documentation and communication
  • Two weeks post-launch support included
  • Clear scope management (changes documented and approved)

We're not just delivering apps - we're delivering confidence.

5. Prove value upfront (risk reversal)

Most freelancers: "Pay me and I'll build it." Us: "Let me prove capability first, completely free."

The App Launch Blueprint costs us ~4-6 hours of work. But it:

  • Eliminates tire-kickers (they won't invest time if not serious)
  • Proves our strategic thinking before they spend budget
  • Catches scope issues before development starts
  • Builds trust that we actually know what we're doing
The result: Zero disputes, zero refund requests, 100% Job Success Score.

Why we beat the 50+ person agency

Last month, we won a major 300+ hours project against an established agency with 50+ engineers.

They pitched: "We have a large team, proven process, extensive portfolio." We pitched: "I'm a founder myself. I've built 4 startups and 20+ apps. I understand the pressure you're under - needing to validate quickly without blowing your runway. Here's exactly what I'd build first and why."

They chose us because:

  • Founder-level strategic thinking (not junior devs following orders)
  • Direct communication with the person actually building (no project manager middleman)
  • Better approach to their specific problem (we understood their business, not just their tech requirements)
  • Faster decision-making and iteration (no corporate bureaucracy)
  • We just kill at designing products, and client saw that upfront
Size doesn't win. Understanding wins.

Upwork isn't dead - it's just selective

The platform works if you:

  • Position yourself clearly with specific expertise
  • Qualify clients before proposing (say no to red flags)
  • Lead with strategy and value, not just technical skills
  • Deliver world-class experience consistently
  • Use upfront value to prove capability
What doesn't work:
  • Generic profiles trying to do everything
  • Racing to bottom on price
  • Copy-paste AI proposals
  • Taking every project to "build reviews"
  • Competing on price instead of quality

We're doubling down on Upwork in coming months as one of our key channels. Not because the platform changed. Because we figured out how to stand out.

---
